On Mon, 19 Apr 2027 at 09:14, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Thanks again for the RFP review call last week...


From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
To: Sofia Ramos <sofia.ramos@atlasbank.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 21 Apr 2027 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Redwood onboarding proposal + high-level phasing for Atlas Bank

Hi Sofia / Ben —

Following up with technical clarifications and a short integration checklist. I’m attaching integration-endpoints.csv with the endpoints and expected payload samples. Highlights: 

- Integration steps (high level):
  1) Network peering & firewall rules (CloudBridge to provision peers).
  2) Service account creation + least-privilege keys for telemetry/write access.
  3) Prefix/KV cache alignment for your tokenization layer (we'll provide a script to validate).
  4) Shadow traffic run for 72 hours before Pilot canary (validation script + baseline metrics).

- Canary windows: we recommend a minimum 48-hour steady-state observation per shard for services with high load variability. The two-week Pilot is conservative and allows rolling canaries across shards with designated business hours.

- Rollback edge cases to note:
  • If downstream pipelines are rate-limited, automatic rollback may still leave backlog growth — we recommend a pre-cutover dead-letter handling plan.
  • If network packet loss >1% during canary, promote immediate hold and escalate to CloudBridge for routing remediation.

Attaching integration-endpoints.csv (CSV) with endpoint URIs and sample payloads. I'm available for a technical sync next Thu 2027-04-29 10:00 PT.

Thanks,
Priyom Das
Solutions Engineering, Redwood Inference
priyom.das@redwood.ai

title: SI-cofunded trial credit routing & redemption matrix
preview:
From: Maya Singh <maya@nimbleworks.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Cofunded trial credits for CloudMarket listing - routing ask

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the intro call yesterday. NimbleWorks is ready to list Redwood on CloudMarket next month and we'd like to co-fund trials for our mutual customers. Before we finalize the marketplace listing, legal and billing asked for a short routing plan: how customers redeem SI-sponsored credits vs Redwood promotional credits, and whether credits can be top-up applied in the marketplace flow.

Attached is a draft "credit-allocation-matrix.xlsx" showing three scenarios (new customers, proof-of-concept, and expansion pilots) and proposed redemption windows (30/60/90 days). Can you confirm Redwood can: 1) accept partner-issued voucher codes that map to a marketplace trial product, and 2) apply partner cofunding first, with Redwood covering any remaining credits up to the trial cap?

If that flow works, we can get procurement to sign the co-funding agreement and push the DocuSign envelope. If not, please advise what changes you need (routing by HubSpot deal id, different expiration, etc.).

Thanks — looking to finalize by next Tuesday to meet marketplace deadlines.

Best,
Maya
Partnerships Manager, NimbleWorks

Attachment: credit-allocation-matrix.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Maya Singh <maya@nimbleworks.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cofunded trial credits for CloudMarket listing - routing ask

Maya —

Appreciate the matrix; clear and very helpful. Short answers below, longer notes after:

1) Partner-issued voucher codes: yes, we can support external partner voucher codes via the marketplace SKU flow, provided we receive the code list and mapping to HubSpot deal ids before listing.
2) Routing order: we can prioritize partner cofunding first, then Redwood credits to top up to the trial cap. We implement this via a two-step redemption call (partner-code validate -> apply partner credit; if remaining balance, apply redwood-promocode).

Longer notes / operational constraints:
- Voucher expiry must be a hard date; our marketplace instrument enforces expiration server-side.
- For auditing we need a CSV of issued codes with partner contact and intended HubSpot deal id.
- Top-ups: Redwood promo top-ups are allowed up to a per-account limit; we'll flag any top-ups above $X for manual approval.

Ben, Jordan — tagging you to confirm the approval channel for manual top-ups and whether we need a special SLA on activation times for marketplace redemptions.

I can loop legal if NimbleWorks needs the short consent clause in the cofunding agreement.

Thanks,
Ava
Ava Chen
Partnerships - Redwood Inference

Quoted: 
> On Thu, 22 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700, Maya Singh <maya@nimbleworks.com> wrote:
> > [attachment: credit-allocation-matrix.xlsx]

Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 13:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Cofunded trial credits for CloudMarket listing - routing ask

Ava / Jordan —

Good with the flow in principle. A few operational points from billing:
- We need the partner-issued codes CSV (code, amount, expiry, external_contact, related_hubspot_deal_id) at least 48 hours before a batch goes live.
- Manual top-up approvals should be handled by CS for amounts > $2k; assign to Jordan's queue (CS-Partner-Topups).
- Please note: marketplace redemptions currently run nightly; if NimbleWorks needs same-day activation we'll need to route through an activation webhook (adds complexity).

Jordan — can you confirm whether CS can accept the 48-hour SLA and pick up top-ups > $2k? If not, we should set expectations with NimbleWorks.

Thanks,
Ben
Head of Revenue Ops, Redwood Inference

title: Brightline Labs — temporary invoice-only onboarding & compliance checklist
preview:
From: Marta Ruiz <marta@brightline.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example.com>
Cc: procurement@brightline.io
Date: 2027-04-26T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Quick question on PO requirement for pilot

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team loved the low-latency routing examples. We’re lined up to start a 6-week technical pilot next Monday but hit a snag with our procurement team: our accounts payable process requires a signed PO for any vendor onboarding.

Because this is a short technical pilot and we’ve agreed to capped spend (~$6k), procurement is asking whether Redwood can accept invoice-only terms for the pilot period and provide some form of evidence/certification (SOC2 attestation, data flow diagram) for their record.

Can you confirm whether Redwood supports a temporary invoice-only onboarding for new pilots, and what evidence you can provide? If needed I can loop in our contracts person.

Attaching our standard onboarding form for your intake team (Brightline_Onboarding_Form_v1.pdf).

Appreciate a quick note — we need the procurement sign-off by EOD tomorrow to keep the Monday start.

Best,
Marta Ruiz
Head of IT Procurement
Brightline Labs

---
Attachment: Brightline_Onboarding_Form_v1.pdf (application/pdf)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <marta@brightline.io>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.example.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwood.example.com>
Date: 2027-04-26T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick question on PO requirement for pilot

Hi Marta — thanks for the heads up and for sharing the onboarding form.

Short answer: we can support a temporary invoice-only onboarding for pilots under a controlled exception, provided Finance signs off and we collect a minimal set of compliance artifacts.

What we need from Brightline to proceed (quick checklist):
- Signed pilot statement of work or MOU (term, spend cap, duration) — a simple DocuSign is fine.
- SOC 2 report OR link to your vendor security page + point of contact (if SOC2 not available).
- Completed onboarding form (you already shared this — thanks).
- Primary billing contact and PO/Invoice routing email.

Operational constraints from our side: temporary exception limited to one invoice-only window for the pilot (max $10k). After pilot we’ll require PO for ongoing purchases. The exception will be recorded in our procurement tracker and a brief finance approval XLS (attached) will be created.

I’m looping in Priya (Sales Ops) to help collect the onboarding answers and Naomi in Finance to request the formal exception. Priya — can your team intake Brightline_Onboarding_Form_v1.pdf into the vendor portal and confirm the missing fields by 3pm PT? Naomi — any initial red flags on the $6k pilot? If you’re okay I’ll ask Marta for the MOU link and Bill-to contact.

Next steps:
1) Priya to confirm intake checklist items.
2) Brightline to send MOU link or DocuSign.
3) Naomi to confirm temporary exception approval (email is fine).

-Ben

title: TrustCenter adoption — impact assessment and procurement timeline
preview:

To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: TrustCenter adoption — impact assessment and procurement timeline

Hi Marissa / Stephanie,

We need a short impact assessment for adopting TrustCenter's Trust Portal across our hosted and private customers. This is coming up because the current Vanta contract for our site-wide subscription expires in July and we need a clear recommendation (keep Vanta, move to TrustCenter, or hybrid) with procurement steps. Please include cost delta, implementation effort, and any compliance/regulatory risks for EU customers.

Specific asks: 
- Marissa: can you run a quick cost model (annual TCO) comparing our current Vanta spend + SRE integration hours vs the TrustCenter quote (attached) and a hybrid where we keep Vanta for Dedicated accounts?
- Stephanie: can you map expected evidence collection gaps for our private deployment product (I've attached a starting evidence-gap matrix) and estimate internal engineering hours to remediate (per-control basis).

Timeline: I need a decision recommendation + redlined POs/contract points to legal by EOD Friday (2027-04-23) so procurement can vendor-lock by the end of May if we choose to switch before July.

Attachments: trustcenter-pricing-quote-2027-04.pdf, evidence-gap-matrix-2027-04.xlsx

Thanks —
Jordan
Jordan Blake
Head of Platform Partnerships, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Apr 2027 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: TrustCenter adoption — impact assessment and procurement timeline

Jordan,

Thanks — I'll take the cost model. First-pass observations after skimming the quote (Alicia's packet):
- TrustCenter price is seat + connectors + evidence storage; they list an optional SOC2 automation add-on that replaces ~40% of manual evidence work for integrations we already use (SaaS connectors).
- For Dedicated customers (redwood-dedicated) there is an extra per-environment agent license. That will push cost up if we onboard >10 private clusters.

Next steps for me:
- Model A: status quo (renew Vanta) — project current growth and 10% annual price increase, include estimated SRE integration time (40 hours per private cluster initial).
- Model B: switch to TrustCenter — include migration labor (estimated 120 total hours from engineering + 20 hours from security), one-time connector setup, and recurring license fees.
- Model C: hybrid — Vanta for Dedicated accounts, TrustCenter for Hosted.

I can get numbers back by EOD Thu (2027-04-22). Any additional cost levers you want me to include (e.g., multi-year discount assumptions, early-termination credits from Vanta)?

-Marissa

Redwood Inference

To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 08:23:00 -0700
Subject: Re: TrustCenter adoption — impact assessment and procurement timeline

Hi Jordan / Marissa / Stephanie,

- Pricing in the attached PDF assumes a single enterprise workspace. For multi-tenant or per-cluster agents there are incremental agent fees; if you expect >8 private clusters we'll recommend an enterprise cluster bundle discount.
- Evidence storage is billed by GB/month; if you plan to retain raw logs for >12 months we'll need to estimate that separately (we've included an example calculation on page 6).
- We support EU data residency for audit artifacts (we can segregate EU artifacts to our EU storage; additional fees apply).

From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Apr 2027 11:38:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: TrustCenter adoption — initial gap analysis

Forwarding my notes after working through the evidence-gap matrix: (see quoted original matrix below)

Key findings (short):
- Hosted API: 70% of our current controls map to TrustCenter connectors out-of-the-box; remaining 30% are custom (pipeline-level access logs, ephemeral Kubernetes job artifacts).
- Private (VPC/on-prem): only ~45% mapped; the rest require either agent deployment or custom S3/syslog forwarding. That means engineering effort per private environment is higher than the TrustCenter quote assumes.
- Major risk: EU customers with strict data residency and deletion windows will need per-customer retention configs. TrustCenter supports that, but it will push us into the EU storage add-on tier.

- Implement agent-based evidence collection for 12 private clusters: 12 * 40h = 480 engineering hours (can be phased).

title: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: secops@acme-acme.com, devops@acme-acme.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints

Hi team,

Thanks for setting up time next week to review the private deployment handoff. Before the call, two asks so we can make the session actionable:

1) Can you confirm whether your private endpoints will terminate TLS at your edge (customer-managed TLS) or ask Redwood to terminate on a private endpoint inside your VPC?
2) If you expect Redwood to terminate, we need a list of all private hostnames (internal domains) and any SNI routing requirements (per-tenant SANs vs wildcard). A simple CSV is fine.

Notes from our side:
- We support SNI-based routing at our private ingress; that allows per-tenant certs but requires explicit SANs or per-tenant cert uploads.
- For on-prem deployments we also support customer-provided internal CA bundles and HSM-backed keys (offline signing workflows).
- ACME/Let’s Encrypt is not an option for private internal domains.

Attached: initial cert inventory template and a small diagram of our SNI routing node.

If you can send the CSV before EOD Monday we’ll prepare suggested cert lifetimes, rotation windows, and a proposed mapping table.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com
[attachment: cert_inventory_20270422.csv (csv)]
[attachment: sni-routing-diagram.svg (svg)]

From: Acme Security Team <secops@acme-acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: devops@acme-acme.com
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints

Ben —

Thanks. Quick clarifying questions before we populate the inventory:

- If Redwood terminates TLS inside our VPC, what auth method do you use for the control channel back to Redwood (mTLS, token)? We need to validate that in our threat model.
- For per-tenant cert uploads: do you require PKCS#12 with private key, or can we provide cert-only (public cert) and have Redwood hold the private key in customer's HSM?
- Do you support SNI fallback (e.g., default cert for unknown SNI) or will unknown SNI be rejected? We may have dynamic subdomains during testing.

Also: we have two groups — security and devops. Security will own CA trust decisions; devops will supply hostnames + routing rules once the trust model is set.

—Acme SecOps
Acme Corp
secops@acme-acme.com

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, secops@acme-acme.com, devops@acme-acme.com
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 12:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints

Hi all — answering Acme's bullets and proposing options so you can pick what's best for your ops/security teams.

1) Control channel auth: we prefer mTLS for the control plane inside customer VPCs; tokens (short-lived) are supported for hosted but mTLS gives auditable client cert mapping. If you choose mTLS we can supply CSR + signing expectations or accept your CA bundle.

2) Cert upload options: three supported modes:
  - Customer-managed private keys: you upload PKCS#12; Redwood stores in our HSM (if you opt-in) and we do not export.
  - Customer HSM (recommended for high security): you keep keys in your HSM and we configure SNI routing to forward TLS to your device (TLS passthrough).
  - Cert-only (public cert): we can accept public certs + OCSP stapling config if you host private key on your side; this is useful for test environments.

3) Unknown SNI handling: we support both behaviors. By default any unknown SNI is rejected with a hard TLS 1.2/1.3 handshake failure (fail-closed). For test sandboxes you can request a default cert for wildcard test.* domains; we'll provision a short-lived wildcard and log the usage.

Additional considerations:
- Rotation: recommend 90-day rotation for non-HSM keys; 365-days for HSM-kept keys, with an automated rollback plan (canary routing) for cert rollouts.
- SAN strategy: per-tenant SANs is the most secure but operationally heavier. Shared wildcard per environment is cheaper but increases blast radius.
- IPv6: SNI works the same; ensure you include AAAA records in your inventory.

We'll prepare a short playbook for these choices once Acme picks 1 of the three cert modes and confirms rotation SLA.

Karthik
Principal Infrastructure Engineer — Redwood Inference

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
To: secops@acme-acme.com, devops@acme-acme.com
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Apr 2027 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints

Hi Acme team — attaching the cert inventory template filled with example rows to show required columns. A few operational checklist items for your security folks: 

Required columns in the CSV we need: hostname, env (prod/stage/test), ip (primary), ip_secondary (optional), ipv6 (if any), expected_sni, cert_mode (customer-key | redwood-hsm | passthrough), cert_expiry (YYYY-MM-DD), contact_owner, notes.

Operational checklist (short):
- Confirm trust root: provide PEM chain for any internal CA you want Redwood to trust (subject + fingerprint).
- Key custody decision: define whether keys go to your HSM or Redwood HSM.
- Rotation window: pick a maintenance window and a rollback window (we suggest 15 min canary, 2 hour fallback).
- Monitoring hooks: we will emit TLS handshake metrics and cert-expiry alerts to your PagerDuty; include escalation contact in the CSV.

Attachments: cert_inventory_20270422.csv (example) and sni-routing-diagram.svg.

Mina Haddad
Security & Ops — Redwood Inference
[attachment: cert_inventory_20270422.csv (csv)]
[attachment: sni-routing-diagram.svg (svg)]

- Do you support SNI fallback (e.g., default cert for unknown SNI) or will unknown SNI be rejected? We may have dynamic subdomains during testing.

Also: we have two groups — security and devops. Security will own CA trust decisions; devops will supply hostnames + routing rules once the trust model is set.

—Acme SecOps
Acme Corp
secops@acme-acme.com

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, secops@acme-acme.com, devops@acme-acme.com
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 12:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SNI mapping & internal CA inventory for private endpoints

Hi all — answering Acme's bullets and proposing options so you can pick what's best for your ops/security teams.

1) Control channel auth: we prefer mTLS for the control plane inside customer VPCs; tokens (short-lived) are supported for hosted but mTLS gives auditable client cert mapping. If you choose mTLS we can supply CSR + signing expectations or accept your CA bundle.

2) Cert upload options: three supported modes:
  - Customer-managed private keys: you upload PKCS#12; Redwood stores in our HSM (if you opt-in) and we do not export.
  - Customer HSM (recommended for high security): you keep keys in your HSM and we configure SNI routing to forward TLS to your device (TLS passthrough).
  - Cert-only (public cert): we can accept public certs + OCSP stapling config if you host private key on your side; this is useful for test environments.

3) Unknown SNI handling: we support both behaviors. By default any unknown SNI is rejected with a hard TLS 1.2/1.3 handshake failure (fail-closed). For test sandboxes you can request a default cert for wildcard test.* domains; we'll provision a short-lived wildcard and log the usage.

Additional considerations:
- Rotation: recommend 90-day rotation for non-HSM keys; 365-days for HSM-kept keys, with an automated rollback plan (canary routing) for cert rollouts.
- SAN strategy: per-tenant SANs is the most secure but operationally heavier. Shared wildcard per environment is cheaper but increases blast radius.
- IPv6: SNI works the same; ensure you include AAAA records in your inventory.

We'll prepare a short playbook for these choices once Acme picks 1 of the three cert modes and confirms rotation SLA.

Karthik
Principal Infrastructure Engineer — Redwood Inference

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>

Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Apr 2027 08:45:00 -0700

Required columns in the CSV we need: hostname, env (prod/stage/test), ip (primary), ip_secondary (optional), ipv6 (if any), expected_sni, cert_mode (customer-key | redwood-hsm | passthrough), cert_expiry (YYYY-MM-DD), contact_owner, notes.

Operational checklist (short):
- Confirm trust root: provide PEM chain for any internal CA you want Redwood to trust (subject + fingerprint).
- Key custody decision: define whether keys go to your HSM or Redwood HSM.
- Rotation window: pick a maintenance window and a rollback window (we suggest 15 min canary, 2 hour fallback).
- Monitoring hooks: we will emit TLS handshake metrics and cert-expiry alerts to your PagerDuty; include escalation contact in the CSV.

title: Ascend AI renewal & onboarding credits
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Peter Lang <peter@ascend.ai>

Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Ascend AI renewal & onboarding credits

Hi Peter —

Great catching up earlier this week. Per our conversation, I wanted to outline a renewal framework that supports Ascend's planned capacity growth while protecting unit economics on both sides. High level proposal: 

- Term: 12 months, starting 2027-06-01.
- Commit: 1.2M inference units (approx. 20% YoY uplift from current run rate).
- Pricing: three-tier discount bands by monthly usage (0-70% of commit = list, 70-100% = 5% discount, >100% = 10% discounted overage pricing).
- Onboarding concession: one-time $40k onboarding credit to offset migration and engineering hours.
- Burst protection: 60-day onboarding waiver for surge overages (no overage charges for first 60 days after go-live), then standard overage rules apply.

We can formalize this as an amendment to the existing SOW with a simple term sheet. Two asks from your side: confirmation on the target commit and whether the 60-day burst waiver covers your immediate production ramp.

I'll loop in Monica and Karthik to finalize legal/finance-friendly language and produce a term sheet. If this looks directionally right, I'll send an updated PDF and DocuSign link by tomorrow.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Monica / Karthik —
Quick summary and recommended internal posture before I respond to Peter/procurement:

1) They asked for
   - 6-month price hold -> escalate to finance: we can offer a 6-month effective price stability (no increases mid-term) if they take 12-month commit; alternatively, for multi-year commit we can extend more favorable pricing cadence.
   - Convert $40k onboarding credit to invoice credit -> low friction, acceptable.
   - 90-day burst waiver in exchange for multi-year -> we should treat 90-day as a concessional ask tied to a 24-month partial commit (e.g., initial 12-month commit + optional 12-month extension with predefined commit bands).

2) Proposed counter (simple, finance-friendly):
   - Keep 12-month primary term starting 2027-06-01.
   - Onboarding credit: $40k applied as invoice credit on first month (no accumulated rebate accounting).
   - Burst waiver: 60 days standard waiver on single-year; extend to 90 days if they sign a 24-month master amendment with a 10% discount step on months 13-24 (relative to months 1-12).
   - Price hold: commit to no upward pricing changes for first 6 months; add a clause to re-evaluate unit pricing at month 7 based on usage bands.

Notes: This protects our economics (90-day waiver for multi-year only) and gives them the predictability procurement needs. If approved, I can draft the small amendment and the DocuSign envelope.

- Ben

title: Post-demo: TTFT & streaming targets (recap + next steps)
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <psingh@stellarpay.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: TTFT & streaming targets (recap + next steps)

Priya — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our call and proposed next steps so we can align on the pilot scope. Please confirm any corrections.

High-level ask from Stellar: ensure streaming responses meet production TTFT and mobile UX targets for your payments flows. From the call I captured the following target envelope you want to validate in a POC: 
- TTFT (time-to-first-token): <= 150 ms median, <= 250 ms p99 (mobile, US region)
- Baseline throughput: 50 request/s (avg), burst to 200 r/s when queued transactions spike
- Concurrency: support ~1k concurrent chat sessions initially; scale to 3k over 90 days
- Chunking / cadence: initial payload (first chunk) within 120 ms of request; subsequent chunks every ~125–200 ms to keep UI streaming smooth
- Preferred transport: SSE for server-driven flows (simpler retry model), but can accept WebSocket for mobile sessions if you require bi‑directional low-latency edits

Technical clarifications we need from you to size the pilot and runtime config: 
1) Typical prompt + response token sizes (avg and 95th pct) for the payments flow.
2) Expected payload size (MB) and whether you include attachments (receipts) in the streamed response.
3) Spike profile: how long do bursts last (seconds/minutes) and expected frequency per day/week.

Proposed next steps: 
- Ben: build a lightweight harness that simulates your mobile client with both SSE and WS options, capture TTFT & chunk cadence. Target delivery: 4 business days.
- Laura: run a 2-day sandbox pilot with a 50 r/s steady-state and two burst windows (5 min, 15 min). Schedule window options in calendar.
- Priya / Prod Team: send sample payloads and a small dataset (anonymized) we can use for token-sizing and latency profiling.

Attachments: SLO_proposal_Stellar_v1.pdf (proposed SLO table + fallback policy), demo-recording-20270422.mp4 (full demo).

If the targets above look off from your expectations, call them out and we’ll revise. Happy to jump on a quick 15-minute follow-up if that helps.

— Alex
Alex Martinez
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Priya Singh <psingh@stellarpay.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Prod Team <prod@stellarpay.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 14:12:10 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: TTFT & streaming targets (recap + next steps)

Thanks Alex — good summary. A couple of clarifications from our side: 
- The 250 ms p99 is our hard goal for the checkout/authorization path; for non-critical notifications we can accept p99 up to 400 ms. So your proposed envelope is fine for checkout but please mark the critical path explicitly.
- Typical token sizes (avg): prompt ~40 tokens, response ~180 tokens; 95th pct response ~420 tokens.
- Payload size: most responses are <30 KB; receipts (rare) can be ~120 KB. We do not stream binary attachments in the SSE stream — we hand off a URL after the final token.
- Spike profile: bursts usually last 2–6 minutes during regional promotions; we see 3–4 such bursts per week.

On transport: mobile engineering prefers WebSocket because we already have a persistent socket and want to avoid negotiating SSE on intermittent networks. However, we are open to SSE for web UI. For the POC, could you run both transports and show comparative TTFT/packetization numbers?

We also need to thread security and compliance: please include your SOC2 reference and a short note on how cached prompts are isolated per-tenant. Our security lead will request a questionnaire soon.

I can share a small anonymized set of 200 sample prompts/responses by EOD. Looking forward to the pilot dates.

— Priya
Priya Singh
Head of Product 
Stellar Payments

Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>

- Ben will deliver two harness variants (SSE + WebSocket) and report median/p95/p99 TTFT, initial-chunk timing, and chunk cadence. Expect: harness + initial results in 4 business days (target Tue, Apr 27).
- Laura will propose two pilot windows next week for the 2-day sandbox (we should aim for one that includes a weekday promo spike to observe realistic bursts).
- Security: attaching the SOC2 summary page in the shared Drive link; Ben, please note and be ready for the questionnaire.

Action items for Stellar (Priya / Prod Team): please upload the 200 anonymized samples to the Drive link and confirm which path you want to treat as critical (checkout confirmed).

preview:
From: Danielle Price <danielle@foresightlabs.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ravi Shah <ravi.shah@foresightlabs.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 15:07:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Quick follow-up + sample prompts from today's call

Thanks again for the demo today — appreciate the concise walkthrough. Attaching a small set of customer prompts we captured during research (foresight_sample_prompts.csv). High level follow-up items for us:

- We're primarily evaluating model fit for: customer support summarization, policy classification (safety flags), and a short answer bot for internal docs.
- Our rough SLA target: P95 latency under 400ms for single-turn short answers; for streaming summaries we care about tail latency and throughput for batch processing.
- Data sensitivity: some prompts include PII-like strings (emails, order IDs). We need guidance on redaction and how Redwood suggests collecting examples safely.

Could you share recommended model targets for an evaluation (balanced cost vs quality), a link to any relevant benchmark pages, and a short list of the exact prompt/edge-case examples you want from us?

Also looping procurement on this thread (procurement@foresightlabs.com) — they asked for a checklist for security/compliance artifacts we should expect during POC.

Attachments: foresight_sample_prompts.csv (CSV, 42 rows)

Best,
Danielle
Director of Product
Foresight Labs


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Danielle Price <danielle@foresightlabs.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 16:12:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Quick follow-up + sample prompts from today's call

Danielle — thanks for sending these over and for the clear priorities. Quick recap + next steps from our side: 

Summary of what we recommend for your POC evaluation:

1) Model targets (recommended starting set):
   - redwood-13b-mixq (balanced quality/cost) — good baseline for summarization and short answers
   - redwood-3b-q4 (cost-optimized) — run on representative high-volume routes for throughput checks
   - meta-70b-std (high-quality reference) — use small sample slices for fidelity checks only (not production throughput)

2) Benchmarks and links:
   - Redwood benchmark dashboard (throughput/latency): https://console.redwood.ai/benchmarks/throughput/bench-2027-Q1
   - Example eval harness we recommend (prompt-based): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ForesightPromptPack

3) Prompt / edge-case collection guidance we need from you:
   - ~200 representative prompts per use case (support summary, policy classification, short answer). Split by typical/edge-case/intent-ambiguous examples.
   - Redaction: replace PII tokens with <REDACTED:EMAIL> / <REDACTED:ORDER_ID> and include an additional column with a boolean 'contains_pii' flag.
   - Annotate desired output for a ~25% sample so we can run automatic regression checks.

4) Quality/regression concerns to watch for during the POC:
   - Instruction-following drift after few-shot prompts — we'll run a 'control prompt' suite weekly to detect regressions.
   - Hallucination risk on internal doc short answers — include golden responses for a subset of 100 question-answer pairs.

5) Logistics & timeline:
   - If you can deliver the prompt pack and redaction flags by Tue 04/27, we can load into our eval harness and provide a preliminary model comparison by Fri 04/30.
   - Procurement checklist (initial): SOC2 report, data retention policy, schema for logs. I'll attach a short prep checklist.

Attachment: ava_prep_checklist.pdf (one-page POC intake checklist)

I'm cc'ing Aditya and Monica — Aditya will add the redaction guideline template; Monica will coordinate the eval harness run once we receive the pack.

Would you prefer the prompt pack uploaded to the Drive folder you shared, or would you like us to create a secure upload link?

Best,
Ava Chen
Principal Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Danielle Price <danielle@foresightlabs.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 17:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: prompt pack request + recommended model targets for evaluation

Quick redaction and labeling guidance from my side — attaching a minimal template (redaction_guidelines_v1.md). A few practical notes: 

- For PII that is structural (order IDs, invoice numbers), replace with <REDACTED:ID_TYPE> and keep a separate column 'redaction_token' with the original token hashed (SHA256) if you need traceability for debugging.
- For free-text user messages that contain names/emails, replace only the exact token, do not change sentence structure; include a separate 'masking_reason' column when redaction affects context (e.g., "customer mentions doctor name").
- Edge-case examples we particularly need: ambiguous queries that require contextual follow-up, multi-turn snippets where the answer depends on prior messages, and examples with nested quoted text.

Attachments: redaction_guidelines_v1.md (Markdown, 3 sections)

I'll be available tomorrow morning PT to run through the redaction template with your data team if helpful.

—Aditya
Aditya Rao
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference

title: IvyWorks availability exception & gating — product / ops asks
preview:
From: Emma Park <emma@ivyworks.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: legal@ivyworks.com
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We finished a brief internal technical review and have one request before we sign: IvyWorks needs a narrowly scoped availability commitment for the /chat/generate endpoint during US business hours (M-F, 8am-6pm PT). Specifically we want a contractual p95 response-time commitment at or below 80ms for our production key path for the first 12 months.

We understand this is outside standard terms — we're open to a higher-tier Reserved / Dedicated plan and a short ramp schedule. Can you outline what Redwood would need to agree to operationally and in contract terms? I've attached our SLO spreadsheet and a one-page mutual action plan.

Best,
Emma
CTO, IvyWorks

[Attachment: IvyWorks-SLO-request.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)]
[Attachment: IvyWorks-MAP-v1.pdf (application/pdf)]

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 10:15:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Team — forwarding IvyWorks' ask. Quick summary: customer wants a targeted p95 <= 80ms for /chat/generate during US business hours for 12 months. They're willing to go to Dedicated and accept a ramp. Legal (Priya) will need to weigh in on risk language. Ops/Runtime (Marcus) — can we provide guidance today on whether this is feasible, and what guardrails / runbook items we would require? CS (Grace) — can you add expected QPS and concurrency + any historical traffic patterns?

Goal: have a short internal decision (yes/no + required conditions) by EOD tomorrow so I can respond to Emma with a proposed path (Dedicated capacity + warm-up + canary + formal runbook).

Thanks — Ava
Product, Redwood Inference

> On Tue, Apr 20, 2027 at 9:02 AM Emma Park <emma@ivyworks.com> wrote:
> > (see above)


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 14:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Short answer: doable with constraints. Technical notes below — high level first:

- Feasible path: Dedicated GPU pool pinned to their tenant for business-hours traffic + pre-warmed KV cache + request routing to low-latency nodes in us-west region. Expect realistic steady-state p95 ~60-90ms for small-gen prompts if we (1) reserve capacity for peak concurrency they expect and (2) prevent cold-starts during hours.
- Hard guardrails: we need a clearly defined traffic envelope (QPS, concurrency, prompt length distribution). If they spike outside agreed envelope, commitment pauses until manual approval.
- Operational asks:
  * Pre-warm schedule + synthetic load test during canary
  * Dedicated monitoring dashboard + alerting thresholds for p95 and queue length
  * Incident response SLA for paging on real p95 breach (who to oncall)
  * A fallback model variant with broader latency but lower cost (automated fallback)

Attachments: rough ops runbook sketch attached. If legal requires a monetary SLA credit we need to size it against committed reserved capacity rather than per-token.

I'll need CS numbers from Grace to give capacity sizing. Also: we should avoid blanket "sub-80ms for all endpoints" language — make it specific to /chat/generate with defined max tokens and input size.

- Marcus
Engineering — Serving Runtime

[Attachment: proposed-ops-runbook.md (text/markdown)]

From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Apr 2027 09:46:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Thanks — adding customer context. IvyWorks is a strategic SMB from our Q1 book; current daily usage is ~30k tokens/day with a predictable diurnal pattern. Expected ramp: 2-3x over 6 weeks post-launch. Peak concurrency during business hours hits ~18 concurrent sessions today. Average prompt length: 40 tokens. They are adamant about p95 for the specific path they showed in the demo (short prompt, single-turn).

Notes for negotiation: they prefer a single-line SLO in the contract rather than a long appendix. That makes our engineering ask of a narrowly-scoped SLO even more important. Suggest we offer:
  - Commitment: p95 <= 85ms for /chat/generate (max input tokens 64, max output tokens 128) during M-F 8am-6pm PT
  - Preconditions: Dedicated capacity reservation (3x current concurrency as initial commit) + 7-day warm-up with synthetic traffic + agreed-upon ramp cap and an automatic throttle if envelope exceeded
  - Measurement: Redwood's telemetry on the dedicated pool; daily digests to IvyWorks and a monthly compliance report

If legal is okay with language around "service credit proportional to reserved capacity for verifiable breaches", I think we can close quickly.

— Grace
Customer Success


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 Apr 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Thanks all — flagged legal considerations below. High-level: we can accept a narrowly-scoped availability/latency commitment if the following appear in the contract:

1) Explicit scope definition (endpoint, token limits, business hours).
2) Traffic envelope and failure modes: a clause that excludes outages caused by customer-initiated traffic above the envelope, abuse, or third-party network failures.
3) Limited SLA credit: define credits as percentage of monthly reserved fee (caps + tiers) rather than per-token.
4) Exit & review cadence: 90-day operational review clause to evaluate the SLO post-ramp; right to adjust terms if we see abuse or unanticipated costs.
5) Auditability: Redwood's telemetry will be the source of truth; provide sample report format to customer.

If product and engineering approve the operational guardrails Marcus outlined, legal can draft a short SLO addendum and redline the MAP. I recommend we avoid open-ended language like "guaranteed sub-80ms" without the other constraints.

I'll prepare a first-pass SLO addendum draft once Marcus confirms capacity sizing and Grace confirms QPS ramp.

— Priya
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference

  - Preconditions: Dedicated capacity reservation (3x current concurrency as initial commit) + 7-day warm-up with synthetic traffic + agreed-upon ramp cap and an automatic throttle if envelope exceeded
  - Measurement: Redwood's telemetry on the dedicated pool; daily digests to IvyWorks and a monthly compliance report

If legal is okay with language around "service credit proportional to reserved capacity for verifiable breaches", I think we can close quickly.

— Grace
Customer Success


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 22 Apr 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: p95 availability commitment for chat endpoint (IvyWorks)

Thanks all — flagged legal considerations below. High-level: we can accept a narrowly-scoped availability/latency commitment if the following appear in the contract:

1) Explicit scope definition (endpoint, token limits, business hours).
2) Traffic envelope and failure modes: a clause that excludes outages caused by customer-initiated traffic above the envelope, abuse, or third-party network failures.
3) Limited SLA credit: define credits as percentage of monthly reserved fee (caps + tiers) rather than per-token.
4) Exit & review cadence: 90-day operational review clause to evaluate the SLO post-ramp; right to adjust terms if we see abuse or unanticipated costs.
5) Auditability: Redwood's telemetry will be the source of truth; provide sample report format to customer.

If product and engineering approve the operational guardrails Marcus outlined, legal can draft a short SLO addendum and redline the MAP. I recommend we avoid open-ended language like "guaranteed sub-80ms" without the other constraints.

I'll prepare a first-pass SLO addendum draft once Marcus confirms capacity sizing and Grace confirms QPS ramp.

— Priya
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference

title: Request: deployment surface & risk walkthrough for RFP
preview:
From: Lena Morales <lena.morales@hearthbank.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.example.com>
Cc: Hearthbank Security <security@hearthbank.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: deployment surface & risk walkthrough for RFP

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking our early RFP — I'm coordinating the security questionnaire on behalf of Hearthbank. Before we complete the formal RFI, we need a concise "deployment surface & risk" walkthrough to include in our submission:

- A one-page network diagram showing customer-facing ingress, private routing, and inter-zone egress controls.
- A short description of your isolation model for multi-tenant hosted vs. private deployments (how network, storage, and key material are segmented).
- High-level threat scenarios you consider (data exfiltration via inference endpoints, lateral movement from shared infra, compromised model weights) and your primary mitigations.
- Any evidentiary artifacts you can share (SOC2 scope statement, test-results for network/egress controls) or links to where we can validate claims.

We need this by COB Thursday (Americas hours) to make our RFP deadline. Can you send an initial package and let me know if we should schedule a 30-minute walk-through?

Appreciate it,
Lena
Lena Morales
Senior InfoSec Manager, Hearthbank


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.example.com>
To: Lena Morales <lena.morales@hearthbank.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.example.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.example.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: deployment surface & risk walkthrough for RFP

Hi Lena — thanks for the clear list. We're happy to provide an executive-ready package and can meet for a 30-minute walkthrough if helpful.

Attaching a concise packet: redwood_network_diagram_v2.pdf (single-page diagram), rfi-surface-risk-answers.xlsx (table-format responses to the four scenarios), and deployment-isolation-matrix.csv (mapping of controls by deployment mode). Summary answers below.

Quick summary (TL;DR):
- Architecture: Host the Redwood control plane in our managed region with inference workers sitting in isolated GPU pools per deployment type. Hosted customers share the control plane but run in logically isolated tenant namespaces; Dedicated and Private customers get separate VPCs/pools.
- Network ingress/egress: Customer requests enter via a regional ingress tier (TLS terminated at edge WAF), then traffic is routed over mTLS to inference gateways that live in private subnets. Outbound egress to external APIs is blocked by default; allowlists, per-tenant egress policies, and an egress proxy with TLS interception for scanning are available on request.
- Threat mitigations: KV/prefix caching obfuscation, strict RBAC and service-to-service mTLS, KMS-backed keys with HSM option for Private deployments, and continuous auditing/tracing.

We'll need one internal clarification about your SOC scope — does Hearthbank need copy of our SOC2 Type II report or just the scope statement and control list?

If that sounds right, I can schedule a walkthrough tomorrow morning PST or Thursday morning.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference
Attachments: redwood_network_diagram_v2.pdf (diagram), rfi-surface-risk-answers.xlsx (spreadsheet), deployment-isolation-matrix.csv (CSV)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.example.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.example.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.example.com>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 15:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Request: deployment surface & risk walkthrough for RFP

Ava / Neha — forwarding Lena's ask (below) plus the draft packet Ava attached. A couple of technical clarifications we should include in the client packet before the walkthrough:

1) Network diagram: include both logical and physical overlays — show control plane public endpoints, regional inference pools (zone-level), and optional VPC peering for Private customers. Note the default 'no outbound' posture and the egress proxy flow.

2) Isolation: for Hosted we use tenant namespaces + network policies; for Dedicated we offer per-customer virtual GPU pools with strict IAM isolation and separate persistent storage buckets with per-bucket KMS keys. For Private we should call out customer-managed network attachments and optional HSM integration.

3) Threat scenarios: add brief mitigations mapping: exfiltration -> response (egress allowlist, outbound proxy, DLP hooks); lateral movement -> segmentation + ephemeral instance policies + guardrails; model theft -> encrypted weights at rest, signing of weight artifacts, and attestation for private runtime.

I'll add two example bullet points for attestation / validation: (a) transient test harness we run during rollout (simulated API exfil), (b) audit log export via customer-managed S3 for Dedicated/Private.

If we can incorporate those, the packet should be good for Lena.

-Karthik


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.example.com>
To: Lena Morales <lena.morales@hearthbank.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.example.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.example.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: deployment surface & risk walkthrough for RFP

Hi Lena — attaching the updated package per our internal review. Notes and links follow; happy to jump on a 30-minute call tomorrow (I can do 10:00–11:30 PT).

What we attached and where to look:
- redwood_network_diagram_v2.pdf — single-page combined logical + deployment-zone diagram (see legend for control plane, regional inference pools, and optional private peering).
- rfi-surface-risk-answers.xlsx — direct answers to the scenarios you asked about with references to controls and evidence artifacts.
- deployment-isolation-matrix.csv — per-deployment-mode table: network, storage, KMS, logging, and attestation options.

Selected clarifications (call these 'executive summaries' to paste into your RFP):
1) Isolation model: Hosted — tenants are separated by namespace-level controls, network policies, and per-tenant KMS keys for persistent artifacts. Dedicated — dedicated GPU pools, per-customer S3 buckets, per-bucket KMS keys, and optional private subnets. Private (VPC/on-prem) — full network isolation with customer-managed peering and HSM/KMS integration; we support an air-gapped package for high-regulation customers.

2) Ingress & egress controls: TLS at edge, WAF, and per-route allowlists. Outbound is deny-by-default; we provide an enterprise egress proxy that enforces allowlists and optional DLP scanning. Audit logs for egress decisions are exported to customer-owned storage for Dedicated/Private configurations.

3) Threat scenarios & mitigations (short):
- Data exfiltration via API: mitigation = deny-by-default egress, egress proxy + DLP, per-tenant rate limits, audit trails; validation = simulated exfil tests (reportable).
- Lateral movement from shared infra: mitigation = strict microsegmentation, ephemeral inference workers, mTLS, and RBAC; validation = periodic internal red-team exercises and logs accessible via exports.
- Model theft / tampering: mitigation = encrypted-at-rest weights, signed model bundles, attestation during load-time for Private deployments; validation = signed-manifest checks and optional runtime attestation with customer HSMs.

Evidence availability:
- We can provide the SOC2 scope statement and a sanitized control mapping table. Full SOC2 reports are available under NDA / customer contracts; we typically provide a redacted report to prospective customers in the later stages.
- Network diagram and control mappings are in the attachments above.

Links & next steps:
- Slide walkthrough: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcdExampleLink/network-diagrams
- If you want the SOC2 under NDA, reply to this thread and we will start the sharing workflow.
- Proposed walkthrough times (PT): Fri 10:00 or Mon 09:00 — I can calendar invite once you confirm.

Thanks again, and let me know if you need anything in a different format for the RFP submission.

Regards,
Ava Chen
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 415-555-0123
Attachments: redwood_network_diagram_v2.pdf, rfi-surface-risk-answers.xlsx, deployment-isolation-matrix.csv

title: Dedicated safety-net reservation + autoscale soft-exemption for Brightline pilot
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin_lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Brightline CTO <cto@brightline.ai>, Brightline Procurement <procurement@brightline.ai>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated safety-net reservation + autoscale soft-exemption for Brightline pilot

Team —

Brightline is ramping their pilot next week and has asked for a short-term dedicated safety-net to guarantee latency during demo windows and initial production traffic. Key ask from the customer: a reserved slice plus the ability to briefly burst beyond the reservation without being preempted immediately.

High level request (Brightline):
- Reserve: 4xA100-equivalent GPU slice in us-west for a 3-week pilot window (target start: 2027-04-25).
- Autoscale soft-exemption: allow up to ~50% additional burst capacity for short periods (max 90 minutes per day aggregated) before eviction — we want to avoid noisy preemptions during customer-facing demos.
- Fallback: automatic model downgrade or routing to a smaller variant if the cluster is saturated.

Attached: pilot_capacity_plan.xlsx (details on expected qps/token mix and demo windows). I also uploaded a short runbook draft.

Asks:
1) Kimberly — can we approve a temporary reservation and what procurement steps do you need?
2) Laura/Marcus/Soojin — what ops constraints or guardrails would we need to safely allow a \"soft-burst\" policy?
3) Finance/Procurement — rough cost delta for the burst allowance (for procurement to sign off).

This is time-sensitive: Brightline wants confirmation by EOD 2027-04-22 to lock sales cadence. If we can’t give the full 3 weeks, propose the minimum we can safely guarantee so the customer stays live.

Thanks —
Aditya Rao
Head of Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
aditya@redwoodinference.com


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin_lee@soojin_lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Brightline CTO <cto@brightline.ai>, Brightline Procurement <procurement@brightline.ai>
Date: Tue, 20 Apr 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated safety-net reservation + autoscale soft-exemption for Brightline pilot

Aditya — thanks for the summary and the plan doc. Quick read: this is feasible, but we need to gate with operational and billing guardrails. Proposed compromise to meet the EOD ask:

1) Reservation: we can allocate a 3-week reservation for 4xA100-equivalent, but initially approve for 2 weeks with an agreed extension clause (auto-extend to 3rd week if usage + cost approvals completed).

2) Soft-exemption (burst lane): we can add a \"pilot-burst\" tag to the slice that permits autoscale up to +50% of allocated vGPU for burst windows. Guardrails I require:
   - Burst is limited to 90 minutes/day aggregated, with per-window cap = 30 minutes.
   - Eviction window: if burst usage is sustained past 30 min *and* cluster pressure > 80%, we preempt into the fallback route.
   - Ops on-call notified via PagerDuty when burst triggers; a runbook must exist (please see attached safety_net_runbook.md).

3) Cost & procurement: finance needs a Delta estimate. Aditya, please confirm expected token volumes for the demo windows and daily average QPS so I can route to billing for an estimate. Procurement will need acceptance of the extension clause and an explicit acknowledgement of potential overage billing for burst minutes.

4) Compliance: any PII/data residency ask from Brightline? If not, default to standard hosted controls. If yes, we may need Private deployment (bigger lift).

If everyone is OK with the 2-week immediate reservation + documented extension flow, I can start the procurement paperwork today.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Director, Dedicated Capacity
Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin_lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Brightline CTO <cto@brightline.ai>
Date: Wed, 21 Apr 2027 09:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated safety-net reservation + autoscale soft-exemption for Brightline pilot

Thanks — a few ops notes from runtime/product:

- Implementation approach: add a policy tag (pilot-burst) that adjusts scheduler thresholds for the reserved pool. When burst is requested we: warm KV cache aggressively, extend batching windows slightly (configurable), and permit ephemeral scale-up to the tagged nodes.

- Preemption / fallback: rather than hard eviction we should prefer model routing to a smaller quantized variant after 15 minutes of sustained >75% host load. This reduces customer-visible failures.

- Observability: require a dedicated dashboard (token-level latency + burst minutes) and a short alert that triggers at 60 burst-minutes used (so we can call procurement/engineering). Marcus — can you confirm we can add that alert by Friday?

- Safety: need a 2-hour load-test window prior to the first customer demo to validate KV warm-up and fallback. I propose Wednesday 2027-04-23 02:00–04:00 PDT. If that passes, I can greenlight the full 2-week reservation.

Questions for Aditya/Brightline: what are the demo windows (dates/times) and the expected peak token per second metrics? Please paste into the plan doc or reply inline.

-Laura
Laura Bennett
Product Manager, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

On Tue, Apr 20, 2027 at 11:03 AM Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Aditya — thanks for the summary and the plan doc. Quick read: this is feasible, but we need to gate with operational and billing guardrails. Proposed compromise to meet the EOD ask:
> ...


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@laura_bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin_lee@soojin_lee@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Brightline CTO <cto@brightline.ai>, Brightline Procurement <procurement@brightline.ai>
Date: Thu, 22 Apr 2027 16:44:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated safety-net reservation + autoscale soft-exemption for Brightline pilot

Thanks Kim/Laura/Marcus —

Answers and next steps from Brightline (copied from their PM reply):
- Demo windows: 2027-04-26 14:00–15:30 PDT (internal demo), 2027-04-28 10:00–11:30 PDT (customer-facing).
- Expected peak: 1.8M tokens/hour during demo spikes (~500 tokens/sec peak per instance cluster) and lower steady-state 100k tokens/hour.
- No additional PII/residency constraints beyond standard hosted controls.

Actions I’m taking now:
- Attached pilot_capacity_plan.xlsx with exact QPS/token numbers and a calendar with the demo windows.
- I accept the 2-week immediate reservation with the extension clause — we will sign the procurement amendment for the third week if the load test passes and cost delta is approved.
- Brightline agrees to the load-test window on 2027-04-23 02:00–04:00 PDT. They asked that the runbook be shared with their SRE team (I’ve cc’d their CTO).

Kimberly — please start the procurement paperwork for a 2-week reservation + explicit burst overage billing. Marcus/Soojin/Laura — can you confirm the runbook and the alerting by Friday EOD? If all green I’ll send Brightline a confirmation email EOD tomorrow.

Appreciate the quick turnaround —
Aditya Rao
Head of Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

Attachments: pilot_capacity_plan.xlsx

3) Cost & procurement: finance needs a Delta estimate. Aditya, please confirm expected token volumes for the demo windows and daily average QPS so I can route to billing for an estimate. Procurement will need acceptance of the extension clause and an explicit acknowledgement of potential overage billing for burst minutes.

4) Compliance: any PII/data residency ask from Brightline? If not, default to standard hosted controls. If yes, we may need Private deployment (bigger lift).

If everyone is OK with the 2-week immediate reservation + documented extension flow, I can start the procurement paperwork today.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Director, Dedicated Capacity
Redwood Inference

- Implementation approach: add a policy tag (pilot-burst) that adjusts scheduler thresholds for the reserved pool. When burst is requested we: warm KV cache aggressively, extend batching windows slightly (configurable), and permit ephemeral scale-up to the tagged nodes.

- Preemption / fallback: rather than hard eviction we should prefer model routing to a smaller quantized variant after 15 minutes of sustained >75% host load. This reduces customer-visible failures.

- Observability: require a dedicated dashboard (token-level latency + burst minutes) and a short alert that triggers at 60 burst-minutes used (so we can call procurement/engineering). Marcus — can you confirm we can add that alert by Friday?

- Safety: need a 2-hour load-test window prior to the first customer demo to validate KV warm-up and fallback. I propose Wednesday 2027-04-23 02:00–04:00 PDT. If that passes, I can greenlight the full 2-week reservation.

Hi all — quick ML-engineering notes to make the kickoff productive. A few specifics we will want to lock during the SE session:

1) Model variants and quality gates
- We’ll propose 2 model families: a high-throughput quantized variant (low cost) and a high-quality FP16 variant for edge-case tests. Define explicit acceptance criteria (e.g., BLEU/ROUGE thresholds, hallucination rate on a labeled subset).

2) Tokenization & preprocessing
- Confirm tokenizers, any domain-specific token additions, and normalization rules. Differences here change token counts and cost estimates.

3) KV cache behavior and session anchoring
- If you plan multi-turn flows, share expected session lengths and whether you want persistent KV caches for active users. This impacts memory and throughput planning.

4) Sample payload sizes and concurrency
- Please provide 3 representative payloads and a target concurrency profile (p50/p95 requests per second) for the first-run.

5) Observability hooks
- We’ll drop a lightweight tracing hook and token-level cost telemetry into your sandbox for the deep eval; we need confirm on log access (S3 bucket or VPC tunnel).

I’ll prepare a short checklist doc for the kickoff and a minimal script to run the week-1 smoke tests.

Cheers — Karthik

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of NovaCorp's procurement review for our platform partners we need a consolidated attestation bundle that covers the following items:

- Current subprocessor roster (name, service provided, data residency/country, contract residency clause)
- CAIQ/SIG mappings for controls that affect data residency and key management
- Copy or link to your latest SOC 2 Type II report (redacted as needed)
- Any residency/exfiltration constraints for on-prem or VPC offerings

I've attached our questionnaire with a couple of focused tabs (residency + KMS). Can you confirm what you can provide by EOD Friday (2027-04-23)? Ideally we receive a single binder or ZIP and a short mapping table for the above items.

Appreciate it — Evan

--
Evan Shaw
Senior Compliance Manager
NovaCorp Procurement
evan.shaw@novacorp.com

Date: Wed, 21 Apr 2027 09:05:00 +0000
Subject: Re: SIG-CAIQ: Third-Party Residency Ledger and Attestation Binder

Hi Evan,

Thanks — got the questionnaire. We can assemble a compact attestation binder that includes:

1) Current subprocessor roster CSV (service, primary location, data-in-transit/residency notes)
2) A residency matrix PDF that calls out which products/datapaths touch EU/US/APAC data centers
3) SOC 2 Type II report (we provide via secure link or redacted PDF)
4) CAIQ mapping document (control-level notes for the residency/KMS controls)

I'm attaching the roster and the residency matrix for your initial review — the CAIQ/SIG binder needs a short internal review but I can push a draft tomorrow. A couple quick clarifying Qs so we scope this correctly:

- Do you need key custody details for each subprocessor (i.e., whether the subprocessor has cryptographic key access vs. no access)?
- For CloudStorageCo (listed in the questionnaire) — are you asking about customer-managed keys (BYOK) or just whether they hold keys on our behalf?

If you can confirm those, I'll loop in Karthik (runtime infra) and Laura (legal) for the CAIQ mapping and the SOC 2 redaction checklist.

Attachments: redwood_subprocessor_roster_20270421.csv (csv); residency_matrix_20270421.pdf (pdf)

Best,
Ben Carter
Head of Third-Party Risk & Vendor Ops
Redwood Inference

Ben — thanks for the quick turnaround, the roster PDF/CSV look good as an initial pass. A few clarifications from the questionnaire:

- For our audit we need explicit KMS custody status for any subprocessor that performs encryption at rest on our behalf. So yes — please indicate whether subprocessor keys are customer-managed (BYOK), Redwood-managed but siloed, or subprocessor-managed.
- For CloudStorageCo specifically: NovaCorp is considering BYOK for object storage. Can you confirm whether CloudStorageCo supports customer-supplied keys in the configuration you use (and whether those keys ever leave our region)?
- For the CAIQ mapping, we mainly need the control IDs that map to data residency and cryptographic controls (e.g., CC6.2, CC7 family) — a short table is fine.

If it helps, we can accept the SOC 2 via a secure ShareFile or DocuSign access link rather than an attachment. The goal is to have something we can present to our security committee by 2027-04-26.

Cheers,
Evan

Evan Shaw
